video_title: MacroVoices #538 Lyn Alden: Is The War Really Over and What’s Next For Markets?
gist: Recorded June 25, 2026 as the Iran/Hormuz conflict appears to wind down, Lyn Alden argues the ceasefire is fragile and consensus, crude has rationally repriced to pre-crisis levels (WTI ~$69, just below its $69.92 200-day MA), and the surprise dollar breakout reflects repriced rate-hike odds. Her north star is that persistent run-hot fiscal deficits (mid-to-upper-single-digit % of GDP, not the Treasury's 4% target) keep nominal asset prices bid; she's bullish on stablecoins as a marginal-but-not-magical Treasury bid, sees the AI/SpaceX trade as overvalued but able to levitate far longer than bears expect, and flags AI energy demand and China's superior power capacity as the key structural risk. Patrick Ceresna's postgame pitches long Dec-2026 natural gas via bull call spreads as the power-bottleneck trade.

insights.claim: Lyn Alden views the Iran/Hormuz ceasefire as fragile rather than resolved, warning the memorandum of understanding leaves enriched-uranium disposition, on-site inspectors, enforcement mechanisms and funding form unresolved, with open questions over whether Iran could re-close the strait after a 60-day period.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: A ceasefire priced as 'over' carries headline-driven reversal risk; oil and dollar positioning sized to a clean resolution is exposed to renewed escalation.
insights.quote: I think this is unfortunately going to be a headline that continues with us probably for at least weeks let alone months because you know that the memorandum of understanding still leaves a ton of details to work out

insights.claim: Crude oil has sold off to roughly pre-crisis levels on both flat price and time spreads, with WTI August trading about a dime below its 200-day moving average at $69.92 — a level that has held the market for essentially all of 2026.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: The $69.92 200-day MA is a clearly defined technical line; a decisive break below it would signal the geopolitical risk premium is fully unwound and possibly oversold.
insights.quote: we're actually below the 200 day moving average, which is at 69 spot 92 on the WTI August chart. And we're only about a dime below that as we're recording.

insights.claim: Alden says she went long energy companies before the war ran them up, chose not to chase them at elevated levels, and is holding rather than adding — a stance vindicated because the feared oil spikes from the long strait closure never materialized.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Illustrates a 'hold but don't chase' discipline on energy equities into a geopolitical spike that failed to deliver.
insights.quote: My view was I'm not really chasing them here. I'm still going to keep holding but I'm not chasing

insights.claim: Alden thinks current oil prices are a near-term rational level if the strait stays open, but that oil trends higher over time, and argues countries and commercial buyers that drew down reserves should be refilling storage and locking contracts now rather than celebrating low prices.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Reserve refilling could become a structural source of physical demand; watch whether an election-year aversion to refilling leaves buffers thin into the next shock.

insights.claim: Alden cites Egypt's April 2026 energy curfew — a forced shutdown of companies at certain hours to preserve natural gas — as evidence that energy-import-dependent emerging markets with little pricing power should lock storage and contracts during the current low-price window.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Highlights tail demand for gas contracts/storage from price-taking EMs; a signal for LNG and gas infrastructure exposure.
insights.quote: back in April, Egypt had to do an energy curfew just because, you know, all these countries are scrambling for natural gas

themes.theme: Fed under Warsh, dollar breakout & rates

insights.claim: Alden argues new Fed Chair Warsh — historically hawkish on both the balance sheet and rates — had to adopt a hawkish-but-vague tone to preserve credibility and avoid looking like a rate-cutting 'puppet,' so the dot plot now signals a possible hike rather than the aggressive cuts many expected.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Repricing of hike odds, not cuts, is the operative Fed-policy assumption; rate-cut-dependent trades are mispositioned for this leadership.
insights.quote: in order to maintain credibility, in order to not look like, you know, a puppet that many people would would criticize him as being... they had to come in and say, well, look, the numbers are high.

insights.claim: Alden expects the Fed to look through the energy-driven inflation spike (as it historically does) and judge policy on non-energy inflation, so if inflation rolls over alongside an open strait and calmer oil, more patient Fed language could return.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Frames non-energy inflation as the variable that flips the Fed dovish; a watch item for rates and dollar reversal.

insights.claim: Alden is on record for a 'gradual print' scenario, fading narratives of imminent large-scale QE; she argues the Fed's balance-sheet task-force options are mostly liquidity-neutral because treasuries must keep being bought to keep repo and short-term funding markets functioning.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Implies a steady, pro-inflationary drift in money supply rather than a QE spike — base case for moderate, not alarmist, debasement trades.
insights.quote: I've been calling it for the gradual print scenario, which is that I'm fading kind of these narratives that there's like a really big QE around the corner

insights.claim: Alden notes that letting banks hold more treasuries via deregulation of capital requirements is, at the end of the day, similar to QE as a pro-liquidity move, reinforcing her thesis that treasuries remain bought and liquid regardless of mechanism.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Bank-capital deregulation is a backdoor liquidity lever; watch SLR/capital-rule changes as a pro-Treasury, pro-liquidity signal.

insights.claim: Alden attributes the dollar index breakout above its 15-month range largely to repriced rate-hike odds plus a still-alive AI trade pulling capital into U.S. equities, but expects the dollar to flatline and trade in a choppy band rather than start a sustained bull market.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Argues against chasing the dollar breakout; a stronger dollar ironically pressures the U.S. economy and self-limits, capping the move.
insights.quote: it's a little bit of a an aggressive move I think in the dollar I wouldn't get in front of it right now

insights.claim: Alden says she watches dollar versus the Chinese yuan and emerging-market currencies more than the euro-heavy DXY, because few investors are bullish on the European economy and the euro is the index's largest comparable.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Suggests DXY strength is partly a weak-euro story; dollar/EM and dollar/CNY crosses give a truer read on dollar strength.

themes.theme: Fiscal dominance & the run-hot north star

insights.claim: Alden takes the over on the Treasury Secretary's forecast of returning to 4% deficits as a share of GDP by the end of the administration, instead expecting deficits to stay in the mid-to-upper-single-digit range alongside high nominal GDP growth and an aggressive clip of Treasury issuance.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Persistent large deficits are the structural bid under nominal asset prices; positions sized to fiscal consolidation are likely wrong.
insights.quote: I'll take the uh over on that. I don't think they're going to get back to 4%.

insights.claim: Alden's 'north star' framework is that fiscal is more powerful than people expect: any trimming of fiscal is a big downside force, while ongoing large deficits make high-quality equities, scarce assets and structural compounders hard to bet against in a run-hot environment.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: A core allocation thesis: own a diversified collection of quality/scarce assets and respect fiscal as the dominant macro driver.
insights.quote: the kind of the north star that I keep airing toward is that fiscal is more powerful than people expect.

insights.claim: Alden argues the U.S. can sustain larger deficits than other countries because reserve-currency status and inflexible global dollar demand — much of it cross-border debt between non-U.S. entities — give it a long runway, requiring a far deeper shock to trigger a true debt spiral than people assume.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Reframes U.S. debt sustainability around currency demand depth; tail-risk hedges should price a slow grind, not an imminent failed auction.
insights.quote: there's all this just persistent inflexible demand for dollars

insights.claim: Alden contends the cost of fiscal dominance shows up not in failed auctions or double-digit inflation but in a two-speed K-shaped economy: asset owners and those on the right side of AI capex or deficits prosper, while young families facing high mortgage rates and housing affordability struggle, fueling populism.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Predicts the deficit consequence is political polarization and rising populism rather than market crisis — a slow-burn risk for policy regime change.
insights.quote: these very large fiscal deficits that are not necessarily directed at the young or not directed at productive things per se

insights.claim: Alden flags that someone long assets and short a 30-year mortgage (or a corporation with 10-30 year fixed bonds) is well-positioned in the current run-hot regime, whereas a young family trying to buy a home is the loser of the same fiscal dynamic.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Concrete positioning rule: be long assets financed with long-dated fixed-rate debt to benefit from nominal inflation of asset prices.
insights.quote: if someone is long assets and short a 30-year mortgage... they're generally pretty happy with the current status quo

insights.claim: Alden, bullish on stablecoins since a ~$30bn market cap in January 2021, notes the market is now around $300 billion and climbing and expects it to eventually exceed $1 trillion before she reevaluates.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: A multi-year structural growth thesis; over $1 trillion is the next milestone to watch for stablecoin issuers and their Treasury holdings.
insights.quote: now it's, you know, what 300 billion and climbing. I I think eventually we'll see well over a trillion in stable coin market cap.

insights.claim: Alden frames stablecoins as mostly yieldless dollar exposure — good for payments and working capital, poor for savings — making them especially attractive to businesses in fragmented-currency regions like Africa (~40 currencies) and Latin America (~30+ currencies).
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Identifies cross-border working-capital/payments as the real stablecoin TAM, not savings — guides where adoption and revenue scale.
insights.quote: they're mostly yieldless products. So anyone holding them uh is getting dollar exposure but no yield... they're good for payments. They're good for working capital. They're less ideal for savings.

insights.claim: Alden argues stablecoins are net-new but marginal Treasury demand: citing a Citigroup analysis with a base case around $1 trillion+ and a bull case near $3 trillion of market cap by ~2030, she notes the Treasury Secretary cited the bull case, and that even $500-750bn of fresh Treasury demand equals only three to five months of U.S. deficits.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Quantifies stablecoins as a runway-extender, not a fix — sizing the Treasury bid against deficit scale tempers the 'stablecoin statecraft' narrative.
insights.quote: If you use you know 750 billion it's at four or five months of deficits. If you use say a full trillion you're getting you know somewhere in a half a year of deficits.

insights.claim: Alden notes stablecoins ironically take market share from other capital pools that already own treasuries, but because stablecoins are required to hold a much higher Treasury allocation, the shift is only marginally net-new Treasury demand.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Cautions against double-counting stablecoin Treasury demand; the net effect is smaller than gross market-cap growth implies.

insights.claim: Alden argues U.S. policy mainly needs to 'get out of the way' of organic stablecoin demand, noting it is bottom-up — Cairo still runs a physical-dollar black market while tech-forward Nigeria has embraced stablecoins heavily — and that the U.S. could kill liquidity by sanctioning the top issuers if it chose.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Regulatory restraint, not promotion, is the key variable for stablecoin growth and dollar network effects; issuer-sanction risk is the tail.
insights.quote: for the most part, the countries just have to get out of the way and kind of let that demand happen

insights.claim: Alden says liquidity is highly concentrated in the top two stablecoins, which have high depth but still less than repo or Treasury liquidity, and warns that rapid stablecoin selling could destabilize the underlying assets — a reason regulators watch the space.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Concentration and run-risk in the top issuers is the systemic fragility to monitor as the asset class scales.

insights.claim: Alden expects the AI trade to continue longer than people think, citing breakout earnings from Micron and high RAM demand for at least several quarters, while warning bears expecting it to roll over next quarter are premature.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Don't short the AI capex cycle on valuation alone; memory/RAM demand has near-term legs and momentum can persist.
insights.quote: I think it continue longer than people think... we just saw breakout earnings from from Micron

insights.claim: Alden uses Tesla as a template for how narrative momentum lets stocks levitate: Tesla has had no real revenue growth in three years yet trades at ~14x price-to-sales versus Toyota's ~7x despite growing slower over that window.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Valuation can stay decoupled from fundamentals for years; shorting on stretched multiples without a catalyst is dangerous.
insights.quote: Tesla hasn't had revenue growth really in in 3 years. And yet, you know, a company like Toyota trades at what, like seven times price to sales, whereas something like Tesla trades at 14 times price to sales

insights.claim: Alden describes a reflexive feedback loop where overvaluation becomes self-fulfilling: an inflated stock price lets a company issue equity to fix its balance sheet, citing Tesla's earlier turnaround and SpaceX's ~$60bn all-stock acquisition using overvalued currency.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Reflexivity means richly valued AI names can entrench themselves via cheap equity-funded M&A — a reason bubbles self-sustain.
insights.quote: there's that feedback loop where the valuation actually impacts the fundamentals. It can shore up the balance sheet

insights.claim: Alden estimates SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI together represent close to $3 trillion of value (with SpaceX over 100x revenue), framing the wave of pure-play AI IPOs as an unprecedented amount of supply the market must absorb, with founder-share unlocks a later selling risk.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A ~$3tn IPO supply wave plus eventual lock-up expiry sets up dot-com-style absorption risk even if the secular AI bet is right.
insights.quote: It's like $3 trillion between the three of of those... That's bigger than the than the United States entire national debt when I was a kid.

insights.claim: Alden agrees AI likely repeats the dot-com pattern — correct long-term bet, but a big bust after the market gets ahead of itself — noting hyperscalers have gone free-cash-flow negative, aggressively issued bonds, and even tapped non-U.S. bond markets to fund the buildout.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Flags funding stress in the AI buildout; an eventual large pullback is expected and Alden frames it as healthy for the market.
insights.quote: we've seen most of the hyperscalers go free cash flow negative. They've aggressively issued bonds. They've even, you know, turned to non US markets to issue bonds

insights.claim: Alden sees little economic moat in AI models themselves due to low switching costs, and is instead more bullish on the harder-to-reproduce bottlenecks in the AI stack.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Steers capital toward physical bottlenecks (power, memory, infrastructure) over commoditized model layers when picking AI winners.
insights.quote: Right now, I don't see a lot of economic moat in the AI models themselves. I mean, as customers have shown, I mean, they're pretty low switching costs.

insights.claim: Alden is building a large watch list of former growth stocks now priced at deep value — including software and consulting names seen as 'wrong side of AI' — arguing core banking-software providers trade at roughly six times earnings despite flat-to-higher earnings growth, and that bearishness on them is overdone.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Contrarian long idea: cheap, growing, fortress-balance-sheet names battered by AI fear may be the inverse of overvalued AI darlings.
insights.quote: the core software companies that provide the software that that US banks run on... they're pretty much all trading at like six times earnings despite most of them still have flat to higher earnings growth.

insights.claim: Alden's repeatable trade framework is to invert the question — ask what has gone up a ton and might be a short versus what has collapsed and might not be dead yet — while explicitly refusing to fade trends that are still trending or catch falling knives.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: A disciplined long/short generator for the AI dispersion: pair overextended winners against unfairly punished survivors, timed to the turn.
insights.quote: always kind of invert the question. So what has gone up a ton and might be a short versus what has collapsed and might still not be dead yet?

themes.theme: AI energy demand & China power advantage

insights.claim: Alden argues AI's next bottleneck is energy, and that China holds a massive structural advantage because its huge power-and-heat industrial base — built for heavy industry — translates directly into the ability to power data centers, while a 10-year lead time on nuclear leaves a near-term U.S. supply gap.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Frames power capacity as a key AI competitive moat where China leads; bullish long-term for energy infrastructure and nuclear buildout.
insights.quote: China has a tremendous amount of power... that's a huge just economic moat that China has built. And then now it it translates to AI just as much if not more than the industrial base.

insights.claim: Alden is excited about AI companies' large investments in advanced nuclear as an accelerant to a nuclear renaissance she considers essential, while cautioning that nuclear takes about 10 years to come true, leaving a near-term energy-supply gap for exponential AI demand.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Long-runway bullish case for nuclear, but the 10-year lead time means natural gas and grid stress dominate the investable horizon.
insights.quote: there's AI companies that are making huge investments in advanced nuclear... Look, it takes 10 years for that stuff to to actually come true.

insights.claim: Alden warns of a political backlash risk where the average consumer feels their electric bill has tripled because of AI data centers and turns hostile, a problem China largely avoids due to both less political freedom and more available power.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Consumer power-price backlash is a regulatory/permitting risk to U.S. data-center growth; a sector-specific political tail.

insights.claim: Alden pushes back on simplistic AI-energy fear narratives, noting some studies show states with more data centers don't necessarily have higher power prices and comparing data-center water usage favorably to crop water use like almonds, while conceding noise, water and socialized costs make siting a state-and-community-level issue.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Data-center opposition is local and solvable, not a uniform headwind; siting risk varies by state, informing infrastructure location bets.
insights.quote: if you compare it to say water for like almonds... how many people at the current time are complaining about water for almonds versus water for data centers

insights.claim: Alden notes both Bernie Sanders and JD Vance have proposed partially nationalizing AI companies via government equity stakes, and argues recent U.S. pressure on Anthropic effectively slows centralized AI players and markets the open-source (often non-U.S., including Chinese) alternatives.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Heavy-handed regulation of centralized U.S. AI labs is a structural tailwind for open-source and Chinese models — a competitive-dynamics signal.
insights.quote: whenever they go too aggressively at the centralized models is basically marketing for open- source versions

insights.claim: Alden flags AI-driven cyber-security risk as a genuine national- and corporate-security concern, noting AI tools can rapidly poke around codebases to find long-dormant vulnerabilities, with particular danger for DeFi where one hack can permanently lose funds.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Rising AI-enabled exploit risk is a tail for DeFi, data-holding firms and corporate security — a watch item and potential cyber-defense demand driver.

insights.claim: Alden and the host agree that building data centers in orbit is unlikely to be cost-effective within a 5-10 year investable horizon, because the physical energy cost of lifting mass to orbit and the lack of in-space maintenance offset the efficiency of space-based solar and natural cooling.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Discounts a key SpaceX bull narrative; orbital data centers are a government-contract story, not a scalable commercial AI-compute solution near-term.
insights.quote: Yeah, I would take the under on how many orbital data centers we have, you know, between now and 10 years from now. So, 2036.

insights.claim: Alden says SpaceX's orbital-data-center economics hinge on radically increasing rocket reusability — reusing rockets 10 to 100 times with minimal maintenance to cut per-lift cost — a near-perfect engineering outcome she doubts is achievable at scale within 10 years.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Reusability multiples are the make-or-break variable for the orbital-compute thesis; a concrete engineering hurdle to track.
insights.quote: a variable that they have to get almost perfect is they have to radically increase kind of the reusability of their rockets

insights.claim: Alden states she generally prefers SpaceX to Tesla within that group of companies and credits Starlink's success, but is not a SpaceX buyer at roughly 100x+ price-to-sales.
insights.speaker: Lyn Alden
insights.so_what: Differentiates business quality (Starlink) from valuation; a not-a-buyer stance on SpaceX at current multiples.

themes.theme: Postgame: nat gas trade & technical levels

insights.claim: Patrick Ceresna's trade of the week is long Dec-2026 natural gas futures — basing along yearly lows — via bull call spreads, to express Alden's AI power-bottleneck theme with defined-risk convex upside on a longer-dated thesis.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: A concrete defined-risk way to play the AI-power thesis; long Dec-2026 gas via call spreads rather than the roll-decay-prone front-month ETFs.
insights.quote: I want to focus on the idea of being long natural gas as a way to express that power bottleneck theme

insights.claim: Ceresna argues UNG suffers structural negative roll yield from rolling front-month futures into contango (especially the winter strip), so UNL — holding laddered exposure across the curve — is often a cleaner delta-1 vehicle despite far less liquid options.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Vehicle selection matters: UNG for liquid options, UNL for cleaner directional exposure — avoids contango drag eroding the thesis.
insights.quote: when the curve is in contango, particularly as it rolls into the winter gas series, that creates a drag on performance through the negative roll yield

insights.claim: Ceresna says the S&P 500 has given back roughly half its post-peace-deal gap-higher rally, with a 10% limit-down move in South Korea's KOSPI spilling into U.S. semiconductors before Micron's earnings beat triggered a relief rally.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Maps the near-term equity tape; semis remain the swing factor for index direction after the geopolitical relief gap.

insights.claim: Ceresna warns index leadership is extremely concentrated with semiconductors doing almost all the heavy lifting and breadth weak, leaving the broader market on shakier footing than the headline index level suggests.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Narrow leadership is a fragility signal; a semis stumble could drag the index despite a stable headline level.
insights.quote: leadership continues to be extremely concentrated with semiconductors doing almost all of the heavy lifting while overall breath remains weak

insights.claim: Ceresna views crude's collapse from the 80 handle to ~$69 as forced-flow liquidation rather than fundamentals, sees the market as extremely oversold short-term, and pegs intermediate fair value around the $80-$85 area as a reversion magnet.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Sets up a mean-reversion long thesis in crude toward ~$80 once forced selling exhausts — a contrarian counter to the relentless sell-off.
insights.quote: this latest leg lower has been driven less by fundamentals and more by forced flows as a large number of traders who are positioned the wrong way are being pushed into liquidation

insights.claim: Ceresna highlights the dollar index breaking decisively above 100 after a ~15-month ceiling, with USD/JPY holding above 160 and EUR/USD breaking 114 support, and flags 102-103 as the next meaningful overhead resistance.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Defines the dollar's technical roadmap; 102-103 is the level that decides whether the breakout becomes a sustained bull move or stalls.
insights.quote: The next key test will come around the 102 103 area where more meaningful overhead resistance may come into play.

insights.claim: Ceresna notes gold is under active distribution — selling accelerating post-FOMC to a fresh lower low near 4,000 — and flags that a break of 4,000 opens a path to the 50% retracement of the two-year bull market near 3,600.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: The 4,000 level is the line in the sand for gold; a break targets ~3,600, defining downside risk in the profit-taking cycle.
insights.quote: if it gives way, then the next major magnet becomes the 50% retracement of the entire 2-year bull market, which comes in closer to 3600
